coredata.dc:title: Preparation and tribological properties of calcium perrhenate as high-performance lubricating additive toward a broad temperature range
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Materials Research and Technology
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Junhai
Zhuang, Weipeng
Yan, Tingting
Liang, Wenfeng
Li, Ting
Zhang, Lixiu
Wei, Xiaoyi
Zhang, Lipei
coredata.dc:description: In this article, nano-sized calcium perrhenate was prepared using the microemulsion reaction method. By the aid of surfactant, the calcium perrhenate was suspended in pentaerythritol ester base oil to serve as a lubricating additive. The tribological performances of the oil containing calcium perrhenate at elevated temperatures were investigated by four-ball tests and ball-on-disk reciprocating sliding tests. The underlying lubricating mechanism of the calcium perrhenate additive was explored using X-ray diffraction, scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, differential scanning calorimetry/thermogravimetry, Raman spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The four-ball test results indicated that the oil containing calcium perrhenate featured lower friction coefficients, smaller wear scar diameters, and lower temperature rising performance than the base oil under an applied load of 392N. The optimum lubricating property was obtained when the additive was at a concentration of 0.05wt%. The tribological performance in a wide temperature range results showed that the friction coefficients of the oil containing calcium perrhenate were slightly lower than those of the base oil at a relatively low temperature range, and decreased as the temperature increased within a high range. The improved tribological performances owed to the formation of a protective layer, which consisted of the shear-susceptible calcium perrhenate additive as well as some native metal oxides that enhanced the layer carrying capacity during rubbing after chemical degradation of base oil. This protective layer acted as a barrier between the rubbing surfaces, preventing them from directly contacting each other at elevated temperatures.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microemulsion
Nano-calcium perrhenate
High temperature
Tribology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Tribological testing procedure at elevated temperatures
Friction behavior over a broad range of temperatures

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The prepared schematic diagram of as-synthesized oil additive.
The XRD pattern, morphology characteristic, particle size distribution and Raman spectrum of as-synthesized powder. (a) XRD pattern; (b) TEM image; (c) size distribution; (d) Raman spectrum.
The FTIR spectrums of PETE base oil with and without surfactant agent OP10.
The optical images of base oil containing different concentrations of Ca(ReO4)2 additive with dispersion treatment after 15 days.
The mean friction coefficients, WSDs and temperature variations of oil containing various concentrations of Ca(ReO4)2 additive. (a) Mean friction coefficients and WSDs; (b) temperature rising.
The SEM images of wear scars lubricated by oil with various concentrations of Ca(ReO4)2.

The friction coefficient curves with time and the mean friction coefficient of different lubricating samples at various temperatures. (a) PETE; (b) PETE+SA; (c) PETE+SA+0.05wt% Ca(ReO4)2; (d) mean friction coefficients.
The width values of worn tracks lubricated by various lubricating samples at different temperatures.
The SEM images of worn surfaces lubricating by PETE base oil at different temperatures. (a) 300°C; (b) 400°C; (c) 500°C.
The SEM images of worn surfaces lubricated with oil containing Ca(ReO4)2 additive at elevated temperatures and the EDS results of the marked regions. (a) 300°C; (b) 400°C; (c) 500°C.
The TEM image of protective layer lubricated with oil containing Ca(ReO4)2 additive after 10mins sliding test at 500°C and the EDX results of the marked region.

The XPS spectra of some typical elements of worn surface lubricated with 0.05wt% Ca(ReO4)2 additive oil after sliding test at 500°C. (a) C1s; (b) O1s; (c) Ca2p; (d) Re4f; (e) Ni2p; (f) Fe2p; (g) Cr2p.
The Raman spectrums of worn surface lubricated by oil with and without Ca(ReO4)2 additive at 500°C.
The DSC/TG curves of as-synthesized Ca(ReO4)2 additive.
The proposed lubricating mechanism of Ca(ReO4)2 as additive at elevated temperatures.

head.ce:title: Preparation and tribological properties of calcium perrhenate as high-performance lubricating additive toward a broad temperature range

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, Shenyang Jianzhu University, Shenyang 110168, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical Engineering, Shenyang Jianzhu University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Test and Analysis Center, Shenyang Jianzhu University, Shenyang 110168, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Test and Analysis Center, Shenyang Jianzhu University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this article, nano-sized calcium perrhenate was prepared using the microemulsion reaction method. By the aid of surfactant, the calcium perrhenate was suspended in pentaerythritol ester base oil to serve as a lubricating additive. The tribological performances of the oil containing calcium perrhenate at elevated temperatures were investigated by four-ball tests and ball-on-disk reciprocating sliding tests. The underlying lubricating mechanism of the calcium perrhenate additive was explored using X-ray diffraction, scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, differential scanning calorimetry/thermogravimetry, Raman spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The four-ball test results indicated that the oil containing calcium perrhenate featured lower friction coefficients, smaller wear scar diameters, and lower temperature rising performance than the base oil under an applied load of 392N. The optimum lubricating property was obtained when the additive was at a concentration of 0.05wt%. The tribological performance in a wide temperature range results showed that the friction coefficients of the oil containing calcium perrhenate were slightly lower than those of the base oil at a relatively low temperature range, and decreased as the temperature increased within a high range. The improved tribological performances owed to the formation of a protective layer, which consisted of the shear-susceptible calcium perrhenate additive as well as some native metal oxides that enhanced the layer carrying capacity during rubbing after chemical degradation of base oil. This protective layer acted as a barrier between the rubbing surfaces, preventing them from directly contacting each other at elevated temperatures.

ce:section.ce:para: Designing materials that deliver lubrication with low friction coefficients and wear rates across a broad range of temperatures has been one of the most difficult challenges facing the tribology community [1–5]. Friction parts used in aero engines, gas turbines and cutting tools and those for nuclear power and aerospace applications are more often than not subjected to extremely harsh conditions. Most of them need to start up at a low temperature or operate across low/high temperature cycles. The mechanical system as a whole must experience both low temperatures during start-up and shutoff and high temperatures during operation. Therefore, finding materials that are effective for lubrication across a broad temperature range is a realistic challenge in actual applications. When the temperature is above 200°C, traditional liquid lubricating oils would lose the ability to minimize friction due to oxidation and chemical degradation [6–8]. Synthetic aviation oil, fluorolube, ionic liquid, and other new functional liquids can raise the service temperature to a maximum of 250–350°C [9–12]. Conventional solid lubricants like graphite, molybdenum disulfide and some soft metals are oxidized and cannot offer optimal friction reduction whenever the temperature is above 400°C in atmospheric condition [13–16]. Some inorganic materials can be candidates for lubrication at high temperatures due to their low shear strength. These compounds, however, are brittle and non-lubricating at low temperatures [17]. In a word, lubricating materials are sensitive to the working temperature; one kind of lubricant alone has a limited service temperature range and is thus inadequate to offer the low friction required for lubrication over a wide range of temperatures. Therefore, it can be a feasible approach to develop a solid/liquid hybrid mode in which the combined effect of multiple lubricants is utilized to ensure optimal lubrication over a wide temperature range. By investigating tribological behaviors of the oil containing serpentine powder at high temperatures, Qi et al. found that the lubricating performances of the solid/liquid hybrid mode were affected by many factors, such as temperature, oil oxidation, and tribological chemical reaction [18]. The research expected that the combination of the serpentine oil additive and tribological chemical products could enable improved tribological performances over a broad temperature range. Achieving this expectation requires that the solid lubricant as an oil additive satisfy the following requirements: (1) having superior lubricating performance at high temperatures, (2) maintaining its softness and shear susceptibility without compromising the antifriction property of the base oil at low temperatures, and (3) helping with the boundary lubrication regime during oil deterioration. Thus, identifying an appropriate solid lubricant is crucial for the hybrid mode to provide effective lubrication over a broad range of temperatures.

Ideal candidate solid materials for high-temperature lubrication can be those with lubrication natures deriving from metal oxides with low shear strength and the ability to overcome the challenge of oxidation. Among these oxides, double metal oxides have stood out due to their shear susceptibility and high ductility at elevated temperatures and have been widely investigated in studies dealing with solid lubricating materials [19–21]. Bondarev et al. designed nanocomposite VCN-Ag coatings, and investigated their tribological properties at high temperatures. The formation of the AgVO3 and Ag0.4V2O5 phases at elevated temperatures leads the friction coefficients and wear rates to decrease significantly [22,23]. The coatings’ lubrication performance is affected by their temperature-activated phase transformations and thermal stability. Moreover, some chromates, molybdates, tungstates and aluminates have been widely reported to be efficient high-temperature solid lubricants, which are prepared directly or indirectly via tribological reaction during friction at high temperatures to form a lubricating layer that reduces friction [24–30]. All these double metal oxides provide excellent properties for lubrication at elevated temperatures, and using them in combination with the base oil can be an effective approach to exploring the hybrid lubrication mode. In addition, the mixture of these double metal oxides with calcium has been widely used as a lubrication phase to reduce friction. The Ni-P-MoS2-CaF2 coatings can maintain low friction at a temperature between 200°C and 570°C, and this is mainly attributed to the combined action of oxides, MoS2, CaF2 and CaMoO4 generated on the worn surface [31]. Other composites like ZrO2-MoS2-CaF2 and ZrO2-Ag-CaF2 exhibit good tribological properties across a wide range of temperatures because of the combined lubricating effect of multiple components [32–34]. Consequently, it is foreseeable that calcium-inclusive double metal oxides in combination with the base oil can fulfill the function of a hybrid lubricant at various temperatures.

At the present work, nano-calcium perrhenate was synthesized by means of microemulsion reaction, and, by the aid of the polyoxyethylene octyphenyl ether (OP10) surfactant, was suspended in the pentaerythritol ester (PETE) base oil to serve as a lubricating additive. The tribological performances of oil mixtures for the steel/steel pair at room temperature and for the ceramic/steel pair over a wide temperature range were examined using standard four-ball tests as well as the ball-on-disk reciprocating mode. The protective layer generated on the worn surface as well as the layer components’ combined effect on friction reduction and their wear characteristics were analyzed and discussed. The present research demonstrated the potential of the Ca(ReO4)2 nano-composite as a powerful eco-friendly approach to improving lubricants’ lubricating performance at elevated temperatures and their overall effectiveness across a wide range of temperatures.

The materials for preparing the calcium hydroxide powders (Ca(OH)2, A.R. >95%) and rhenium powders (Re, >99.99%) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (China). The reagents for preparing hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30% concentration), xylene, cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), isoamylol, and cyclohexane were acquired from Sinopharm Group Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All these chemicals were used as received without further treatment.

1.12g of rhenium powders were weighed, and rapidly poured into 40ml of hydrogen peroxide solution. The mixture was stirred constantly for 15min, during which a large number of bubbles were created and the solution gradually shifted from gray to transparent. The final perrhenic acid (HReO4) solution was prepared until no bubble overflowed. Then, the calcium hydroxide powders were poured into the freshly prepared HReO4 solution, and the mixture was stirred continuously to facilitate the reaction. Once the reaction was complete, the sediments at the bottom were removed using qualitative filter paper, and the aqueous phase of the Ca(ReO4)2 solution was prepared. Then, 2g of CTAB, 2g of isoamylol, and 2g of cyclohexane were mixed under ultrasonic vibration at 30°C as surfactant agent, addition agent and oil phase, and 3ml of calcium perrhenate solution (0.1mol/L) was added into the mixture to produce a W/O structure. The mixed solution was centrifuged at 12,000rpm, and the lower turbid liquid was extracted. Subsequently, the xylene and acetone were used to wash the reagent and remove the surfactant and oil phase. Finally, the cleaning reagent was dried in a vacuum oven at 200°C for 12h. The prepared procedure of the nano calcium perrhenate is shown in Fig. 1 .

The synthetic aviation oil PETE was used as the base oil due to its excellence in thermal endurance, viscosity index, flash and ignition points, and volatility. The basic physical-chemical parameters of PETE are listed in Table S1. Generally, how well the additive disperses in the base oil has crucial implications for the lubricating application [35,36]. Thus, the OP10 surfactant was introduced with supersonic vibration to reduce the surface tensile, ensuring improved dispersion without agglomeration and sedimentation of the additive. 0.05g of the OP10 surfactant and 5g of the PETE oil were thoroughly mixed using a magnetic stirrer for 30min and then ultrasonically mixed with different concentrations (0.01wt%, 0.03wt%, 0.05wt%, 0.08wt%, and 0.1wt%) of Ca(ReO4)2 additive for 1h at 50°C. By doing so, the PETE oil containing various concentrations of Ca(ReO4)2 additive was prepared to achieve a series of frictional measurements.

The phase composition of the as-synthesized product was examined on an XRD7000 X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Shimadzu, Japan) that utilized Cu Kα radiation (λ =0.15405nm) for the 2θ range of 10°–90° with a scanning speed of 5°min−1. Using a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan) and a NanoPlus laser particle analyzer (LPSA, Microtrac, USA), the morphology and size distribution of the product were investigated, respectively. Differential scanning calorimetry/Thermogravimetric (DSC/TG) analysis was performed with an STA 449 F5 thermal analyzer (NETZSCH, Germany). The DSC/TG experiment was conducted in air atmosphere at a heating speed of 10°C/min and a temperature ranging from 25°C to 1200°C. Raman spectra were obtained using an XploRA PLUS Raman spectroscope (HORIBA, France) with an excitation laser line of 638nm to determine the component and characteristic peaks. The elemental compositions of the oil samples with and without surfactant were detected using a Nocole iS5 spectrometer (FT-IR, Thermo Scientific, USA), and the measurements were obtained after four scans at room temperature in the range of 500–4000cm−1.

Using a WWM-1W four-ball tribotester and the ASTM D4172 standard test procedure, four-ball tests were performed. The tribotester consisted of GCr15 bearing steel balls that each had a diameter of 12.7mm and a hardness ranging from 64 to 66 HRC, respectively. With a load of 392N (the Hertz contact pressure was around 650MPa) applied, each of the tests lasted for 30min at a rotating speed of 1450rpm. With a data collection tool that came with the tester, the friction coefficients were recorded onto the computer. A super-depth optical microscope was used to measure the wear scar diameters (WSD) of the lower balls. Prior to each test, the balls were placed into a 15-min ultrasonic bath and cleaned with petroleum ether. For each test, three replicate experiments were conducted to obtain reliable average friction coefficient and WSD values with error bars. The WSDs of the lower balls were optically measured at room temperature using a VHX 1000 digital microscope (Keyence, Japan). The start and end temperatures throughout each test were recorded by the temperature detector linked to the tester. For the wear scars on the lower balls, their morphologies were examined using an INSPECT 50 SEM instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tribological testing procedure at elevated temperatures
ce:section.ce:para: A silicon nitride (Si3N4) ball and a GH4169 superalloy disk were defined as the friction pair for the sake of maintaining the mechanical property of the materials during a wide temperature range. Using a MFT 5000 multifunctional tribotester (Rtec, USA) incorporating a high temperature module, frictional tests were conducted in air atmosphere with a load of 20N (the Hertz contact pressure was around 800MPa) applied at 25°C, 200°C, 300°C, 400°C and 500°C, respectively. Each of the tests lasted for 10min. The Si3N4 ball came in a diameter of 6.35mm and a roughness no greater than 20nm. The GH4169 superalloy disk had a size of 20mm×20mm×4mm and a hardness of 85–87 HRC. The disk was ground using several types of sandpapers varying from 100# to 2000#, and then polished by nano diamond slurry until the surface roughness was below 100nm. The friction coefficient curve was used to analyze the mean friction coefficient as well as the corresponding standard deviations. To attain repeatable and reproducible results, each test was repeated for three times. Using an INSPECT 50 scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI, USA) incorporating an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS), the worn surface was characterized. The microstructure of the protective layer was investigated with a FEI Quanta 200 FEG device equipped with a focused ion beam (FIB). The image of the protective layer was captured using a transmission electron microscope. With an ESCALAB250 X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, TA, USA), which uses a monochromatic Al Kα radiation source, the chemical state of the worn surfaces was determined. Internal calibration of the energy scale was completed with reference to the binding energy of the C1s peak (at 284.6eV.) of a carbon contaminant. The Raman spectra were used to determine the composition and phase of the worn surface of the lower disk after the rubbing tests were finished.

The original phase of the synthetic product was investigated using an X-ray diffraction instrument. The XRD pattern of the synthetic product is shown in Fig. 2a. The characteristic peaks of the product can be clearly located at around 24.6°, 21.5° and 28.3°, which were indexed to the (121),
( 11
2 ¯
)
and (031) planes of the monoclinic phase. All the main peaks were matched well with those of the PDF card (No. 43-0514), indicating that the feasibility of synthesizing pure nano-calcium perrhenate by using the micro-emulsion method. The as-prepared Ca(ReO4)2 composite had a high c/a value, which meant weak interplanar bonding and the lubricity that may occur as a result. The morphology and size of the prepared nano-lubricants were investigated by TEM and LPSA (see Fig. 2b, c). As shown in the TEM micrograph, the prepared products had a high level of similarity in such features as physical appearance, size, and shape. The mean size of the prepared nano-products was found to be around 20nm. The LPSA analysis indicated a narrow size distribution (21±10nm) for the nano-particles. The Raman spectrum of the synthetic product is presented in Fig. 2d. The characteristic peaks located at around 985cm−1 and 909cm−1 were attributed to the fact that the ν1 band and ν3 band vibrated in the ReO4 − tetrahedron. In addition, the peak situated at 342cm−1 was associated with the Ca–O–Ca vibration [37].

The FTIR spectra of the base oil with and without surfactant and the optical image of the oil mixture with different concentrations of additive are shown in Fig. 3 . As shown in the spectrum of the oil with surfactant (see Fig. 3), the peaks located at 2946cm−1 and 2879cm−1 were assigned to the asymmetric and symmetric features of –CH2– alkyl chain in the base oil [38]. The characteristic peak was located at 1740cm−1, which was attributed to the C=O stretching vibration of the eater group in the PETE base oil [39]. The strong peak of stretching vibration absorption occurred at around 3496cm−1, which was attributed to the plentiful hydroxyl group [40]. The absorption peaks at 1458cm−1 and 1384cm−1 were attributed to the symmetric and asymmetric bending vibration of C–H bond in PETE. The infrared characteristic peak at 1173cm−1 was assigned to the –O– stretch vibration from PETE and OP10 surfactant. In particular, the skeletal vibration of benzene originating from the surfactant was identified at 1622cm−1. This suggested that OP10 could be well dissolved in the PETE base oil due to the lipophilic property of the benzene group. The above results demonstrated that the addition of the OP10 surfactant led to a decrease in the surface tensile of the organic solution. This in turn reduced the agglomeration and sedimentation of the additive surrounding the oil and enabled the additive to stably disperse in the base oil. Fig. 4 provides the optical images of the base oil added with different concentrations of Ca(ReO4)2 additive after 15 days under dispersion treatment. As expected, these different concentrations of Ca(ReO4)2 additive were found to suspend stably and be resistant against sedimentation in the PETE base oil after a physical and chemical dispersing process. Generally, the additive could suspend well in the PETE base oil and realize uniform, sediment-free dispersion after dispersion treatment. These results demonstrated that the addition of the OP10 surfactant and physical supersonic vibration could significantly enhance the nano Ca(ReO4)2 additive's dispersion stability in the PETE base oil.

To understand the tribological performances of the as-synthesized Ca(ReO4)2, calcium perrhenate was added into PETE as an additive for lubrication under four-ball test conditions. Fig. 5 a shows the mean friction coefficient and WSD measured after lubrication by PETE with different concentrations of Ca(ReO4)2 additive at an applied load of 392N. It was clear that PETE added with Ca(ReO4)2 as an additive, even at a very low concentration, could result in significantly minimized friction coefficient and WSD compared to pure PETE alone. The mean friction coefficient decreased remarkably when the additive was at a concentration as low as 0.01wt%. Then it continued to fall as the concentration increased, and hit its minimum when the concentration increased to 0.05wt%. When the additive concentration was greater than 0.05wt%, the friction coefficient showed a slight increase with the increase of the Ca(ReO4)2 concentration, but still to a lower level compared to when no additive was added. This slight increase in the friction coefficient might be ascribed to the agglomeration of the additive, which would impede the continuous supply of the oil and the coverage of the oil film [41]. Additionally, the adsorption of the Ca(ReO4)2 additive onto the worn surface could help enhance a lubricant's wear resistance. The minimum WSD value was 415μm when the additive concentration was 0.05wt%, 37.8% lower than it was when no additive was added to the base oil. Meanwhile, the variation pattern of WSD with concentration was essentially the same as that of friction coefficient. In general, the deposition of the added Ca(ReO4)2 additive on the worn surface generated a protective film and had some rough contact areas filled up. This could reduce the direct contact between rubbing surfaces. As a result, the nano Ca(ReO4)2 added into PETE as an additive performed excellently with friction reduction and wear resistance improvement.

The variation of the oil temperature during the four-ball test was monitored by the accompanying temperature measuring device. This had been a well-recognized technique for friction evaluation. Friction has been generally believed as an energy dissipation process. Specifically, nearly all the energy dissipated during friction, i.e., at the time of contact between the sliding surfaces, transformed into heat. The oil and sample temperatures were pushed up by the friction heat accordingly. Thus, the more the energy dissipated, the higher the rise in temperature. The variation of the oil temperature with the additive concentration is illustrated in Fig. 5b. The oil mixture with the additive showed lower temperature variation than the base oil as well as the oil with SA alone. This observation was in agreement with the mean friction coefficient and WSD results. This might be explained by the fact the rubbing surfaces being covered by the nano Ca(ReO4)2 enabled greatly lower friction. The coverage containing nano Ca(ReO4)2 additive on the worn scar could be formed a low shear film providing low friction. The shear was intensively occurred in the protective coverage, which avoided the direct contact between the rubbing pair, thus lower the temperature rising.

Fig. 6 provides the SEM images of wear scars after lubrication using various samples. For lubrication by pure PETE oil, evident damages were observed on the wear scar, mainly including micro-ploughing and abrasion as well as some debris and furrows. When the nano Ca(ReO4)2 was used as an additive for lubrication, the wear scar exhibited minimal furrows and was relatively smooth. This suggested that the Ca(ReO4)2 nano-composite was highly capable of minimizing friction as an additive in the base oil. This could be attributed to the micro-ball bearing effect enabled by the low friction coefficient and the good wear resistance resulting from the adsorption of the Ca(ReO4)2 nano-composite onto the rubbing surfaces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Friction behavior over a broad range of temperatures
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 shows the friction coefficient curves and mean friction coefficients of pure base oil and base oil added with Ca(ReO4)2 additive for lubrication at various temperatures. The base oil had a low friction coefficient value of around 0.11 when the temperature was 25°C, suggesting its applicability as a lubricant between steel/ceramic contact surfaces. At 200°C, the base oil's friction coefficient value increased to 0.19, which indicated that the bearing capacity of the oil film declined due to the fact that the viscosity decreased with the increase in temperature. When the temperature rose to above 300°C, the oil lost the lubricating capacity and at the same time was decomposed into asphaltene, resin, and amorphous carbon. The TG curve of PETE base oil was presented in Fig. S1. The weight loss of the oil at 240°C suggested that the oil began to oxidation and decomposition. This would cause a sharp increase in the friction coefficient value, which reached its maximum i.e., 0.51, at 500°C. The above observation demonstrated that the base oil was incapable of generating an effective oil lubrication film for friction reduction due to its oxidation and decomposition at elevated temperatures. The friction coefficient value of the PETE oil containing OP10 varied with the increase in temperature in a similar pattern to that of the PETE oil alone (Fig. 7b). This indicated that a small amount of OP10 had a slight or even a negligible effect on the base oil's lubricating performance. When the temperature was in a range from 25°C to 200°C, the oil with Ca(ReO4)2 additive had a slightly lower friction coefficient value than the pure oil, which agreed with the results obtained in the four-ball tests. At 300°C, the oil with Ca(ReO4)2 additive had better lubricating performance than the base oil, with a low friction coefficient value of around 0.29. When the temperature increased to 400°C, the oil with Ca(ReO4)2 additive provided a friction coefficient value of 0.26. At 500°C, the friction coefficient value fell further to 0.22, 56% lower than that offered by the base oil. Overall, the oil with Ca(ReO4)2 additive offered a lower friction coefficient value than the base oil across various test temperatures. Therefore, the expectation that adding calcium perrhenate as a lubricating additive could reduce friction across a broad range of temperatures was met. The reasons could be explained as follows. While the friction test was performed between the rubbing surfaces, the contact area with a high temperature and intensive friction heat offered a highly chemically active surface. This facilitated the formation of a cohesive protective layer, which consisted of the Ca(ReO4)2 composite as well as native metal oxides at the interface. Additionally, thermal softening led to a decrease in strength, which made the Ca(ReO4)2 composite prone to shearing when a friction force was exerted, and the friction coefficient was thereby effectively minimized.

For the worn track of the Ni-based alloy disk sliding against its ceramic counterpart, its width values after lubrication by different lubricating samples at various temperatures are shown in Fig. 8 . After being lubricated by pure PETE oil at 25°C and 200°C, the worn track had a mean width of 170μm and 195μm, respectively. The worn grooves had similar values when they were lubricated by PETE containing SA alone and by PETE alone. This demonstrated that the addition of SA had little effect on how the base oil performed tribologically. The worn surfaces’ width values after lubrication by Ca(ReO4)2 additive oil were slightly lower than those after lubrication by the base oil. With the rise of the test temperature, the worn track had a width value of 298μm, 275μm and 263μm, respectively, after lubrication by oil containing Ca(ReO4)2 additive at 300°C, 400°C and 500°C, all lower than when the lubrication was performed with pure oil or oil with SA at the same temperatures. A possible explanation for this observation was that the Ca(ReO4)2 additive got attached to the worn surface under the combined action of applied load and friction heat, which would reduce the direct contact between friction surfaces and plastic deformation to the minimum. Typically, increased plastic deformation would mean larger groove widths [42]. Moreover, sliding-induced deformation as well as the consequential microstructure transformation at the bottom of the worn surface could drive up the friction coefficient. With the ability to minimize plastic deformation, the Ca(ReO4)2 additive was fairly effective for lubrication at elevated temperatures.

SEM images of the worn surface lubricated by pure PETE oil at elevated temperatures are given in Fig. 9 . Some deep grooves and cracks, along with some wear debris, were observed at 300°C. This indicated that the oil film had suffered damages that resulted in a severe wear on the surface. At 400°C, the worn surface of the superalloy disk exhibited severe plastic deformation, many delaminations and some furrows, indicating that the predominant wear mechanism at this point was adhesive wear. When the temperature rose to 500°C, the plastic deformation and grooves became more apparent, and even some wear debris were observed around the edge of the delaminations. This could be attributed to the removal of material by adhesive wear. In addition, as suggested in the EDS results of marked region presented in Fig. S2, tribochemically-accelerated oxidation took place on the worn surface, which produced metal oxides that could serve as a glaze layer. Nevertheless, because they were hard and brittle in nature, these metal oxides were easily removed when a load and a shear force were applied during sliding against the ceramic material [43]. Thus, it was difficult to form a stable protective layer capable of inhibiting friction and reducing direct contact between the counterfaces. Fig. 10 provides the SEM images of the worn surface after lubrication by oil containing Ca(ReO4)2 additive at various temperatures as well as a summary of the EDS results for the marked regions. As shown in Fig. 10a, the worn surface looked smooth, having some slight furrows and narrow grooves along the sliding direction and smeared with a partially discontinuous layer. The EDS results showed that the marked regions contained Ni, Cr and Fe elements as well as Ca, Re and O elements that made up Ca(ReO4)2. This suggested that the addition of the functional additive played a significant role in minimizing friction. At 400°C, plastic deformation and delamination were minimized, and the worn surface was covered by a non-uniform island-like layer mainly made up of native oxides and Ca(ReO4)2, as revealed by the EDS results provided in Fig. 10e. When the temperature increased to 500°C, a compact and uniform layer with some slight cracks was generated to cover the worn surface. The EDS results showed that the layer was enriched by Ca(ReO4)2, which helped with the reduction of friction between rubbing surfaces. Specifically, the temperature rise resulted in thermal softening, which softened the Ca(ReO4)2 additive a point that it smeared easily on the worn surface to form a complete protective layer. Containing native oxides deriving from the metal matrix under pressure, this layer could provide efficient protection for the contact surfaces. Formation of such a smooth compact layer was a main reason for the reduced friction coefficients.

To detect the microstructure and composition of the adsorbed protective layer, the FIB technique was used to obtain the TEM morphology of the protective layer formed after a 10-min sliding test at 500°C on the worn surface lubricated by oil containing Ca(ReO4)2 additive. The results are presented in Fig. 11 . The layer fit firmly with the superalloy matrix oxides, and the interior of the layer had no observable separation and was free of pores (Fig. 11a). As a result, lubrication by PETE with Ca(ReO4)2 additive provided significantly lower friction coefficients than lubrication by pure PETE at elevated temperatures. Due to the polycrystalline and hard glassy phases in their respective SAED patterns, some diffraction spots and rings co-existed with the background halo. According to the EDX analysis, the marked regions were mainly made up of elements Ca, Re, O, Ni, Cr and Fe, suggesting the layer consisted of the Ca(ReO4)2 additive and some native metal oxides (Fig. 11b). The metal oxides were fairly hard and brittle in nature and could therefore act as hard phases to enhance the strength and carrying capacity of the layer. The soft and shear-susceptible Ca(ReO4)2, however, could be easily adsorbed onto the worn surface to help reduce friction. Furthermore, the soft Ca(ReO4)2 would inhibit the removal of hard oxides due to an adhesive shear force generated by sliding against the ceramic ball, which could improve the combination property of the protective layer.

For a better understanding of the tribological properties of the Ca(ReO4)2 additive at high temperatures, the XPS technique was used to determine the elemental composition of the protective layer. Fig. 12 shows the XPS spectra of the GH4169 sample surface after lubrication by PETE with Ca(ReO4)2 additive at 500°C after 30seconds of sputtering. Some common elements were detected, including carbon (C), oxygen (O), calcium (Ca), rhenium (Re), nickel (Ni), iron (Fe), and chromium (Cr). On the C1s core level spectrum, the peak of C1s as a major component was observed at 283.2eV, which was assigned to the C–C or C–H organic group from the PETE base oil or the OP10 surfactant. Another two peaks were located at 284.3eV and 285.2eV, respectively; they were attributed to the C–O and COO functional groups from the organic solvent [44,45]. The environment contamination during the sliding test or XPS analysis, i.e., the sliding or cleaning process, also contributed to these two peaks. On O1s spectrum, three peaks were observed at 529.2eV, 530.1eV and 531.1eV, respectively; they were associated with the native oxides deriving from the superalloy during testing, the ReO4 −, and some oxygen deriving from the functional groups in organic solvent [46]. The Ca2p peak occurred at 346.1eV, revealing the presence of Ca2+ from calcium perrhenate. The Re4f spectrum showed the strong characteristic peak at 45.5eV, which owed to the deposition of the Re element deriving from the calcium perrhenate. An intensive signal was located at 854.6eV, which was attributed to the nickel in NiO or NiCr2O4 [47]. The Fe2p peak was observed at the binding energy of 711.2eV, corresponding to the Fe3+ state realized in iron oxide [48]. The Cr2p peak located at 576.8eV was assigned to Cr3+ ion in Cr2O3 or Cr2O4 2−. Besides, an XPS analysis was made on the sample after lubrication by pure PETE oil at 500°C as a comparison, as shown in Fig. S3. This analysis detected intensive signals, which were assigned to the oxidation state of Ni, Fe, and Cr. The above results indicated that as two different components of the protective layer, calcium perrhenate played a major role in providing lubrication, while the native superalloy oxides played a minor one. Thus, the improved tribological performances could owe to calcium perrhenate and the native superalloy oxides being combined together to form a protective layer on the worn surface and their being transferred to the counterface. This layer could act as an effective barrier to protect the ball and disk from directly contacting each other.

Raman spectroscopy was used to further determine the elemental composition of the protective layer. Fig. 13 shows the Raman spectra of the worn surface after lubrication by pure PETE oil and oil mixture at 500°C. NiO, Fe2O3, Cr2O3 and NiCr2O4 were detected on the worn surface after lubrication by pure PETE oil. The generation of the NiCr2O4 here could be due to the nickel's oxidation in the superalloy substrate and its further reaction with chromium oxides over the course of friction at elevated temperatures. This meant that the native metal oxides, due to its brittleness, could be easily removed during sliding against the ceramic material, resulting in poor tribological performances of the worn surface and high friction coefficients. For lubrication by the oil mixture, by contrast, Ca(ReO4)2 and some metal oxides were detected on the worn surface, suggesting that the protective layer was made up of calcium perrhenate and oxidation products resulting from tribochemical reaction. With the oxides NiO, Cr2O3, Fe2O3 and NiCr2O4, the layer could have higher strength and hardness, which would effectively protect against furrowing wear that might occur due to asperities on the contact surfaces [49]. Meanwhile, the soft Ca(ReO4)2 composite could enable low shear strength and improved plasticity for the layer. Moreover, the presence of Ca(ReO4)2 additive surrounding the oxides in the layer could relieve the removal of the hard oxides during friction. Thus, the Ca(ReO4)2 composite blended with native oxides from superalloy delivered enhanced lubricating capacity at high temperatures.

Fig. 14 gives the DSC/TG curve of the Ca(ReO4)2 composite. An endothermic peak was located at 786°C, which was attributed to the melting of the sample. This suggested that the Ca(ReO4)2 composite had a relatively low melting point. Another endothermic peak observed at 931°C was attributed to the decomposition of Ca(ReO4)2 into CaO and Re2O7 as well as the gas state of Re2O7 that led to the loss of weight. The double oxide of Ca(ReO4)2 could be deemed as a combination of two individual oxides: CaO and Re2O7, whose ionic potentials were 2 and 12.5, respectively. Typically, a greater difference in the ionic potential would mean a lower melting point and softer double oxides. This might be the reason why the soft Ca(ReO4)2 composite with a low melting point could enhance the lubricating capability of the base oil at low temperatures [50]. With the increase of the ambient temperature, the Ca(ReO4)2 composite was further softened and would easily get smeared on the worn surface, thus enabling low friction at the high temperature range. Moreover, when with an evident ionic potential difference, the double oxide tended to have a highly stable composite structure, causing lower attraction at the sliding contact interface. This would reduce adhesive wear and consequently minimize the friction. At a given scope of temperature, the Ca(ReO4)2 composite as a solid lubricating additive could match well with the base oil without compromising the lubricating capacity and thermal stability of the latter. This composite could also form a highly compressive layer with native superalloy oxides on the outmost surface at elevated temperatures, gaining the ability to bear large amounts of applied load. By contrast, the double metal oxides NiCr2O4 produced by tribochemical reaction at high temperatures could be considered as two mixed single oxides of NiO and Cr2O3, which have the ionic potential of 2.8 and 4.8, respectively. Therefore, the difference in ionic potential of NiCr2O4 oxide is relatively small, which makes little contribution to the lubrication according to the relationship between the ionic potential and the lubricating property of oxides at high temperatures. Fig. S4 provides the XRD patterns of designed calcium perrhenate after 10min of heat treatment at elevated temperatures. It could be clearly observed that the calcium perrhenate could retain its initial phase and crystal structure at room temperature even though the temperature went up. This suggested that calcium perrhenate could maintain high thermal stability and high chemical durability prior to melting or decomposition, meaning that it had the quality required as a highly efficient lubricant additive.

Based on the above test results and analysis, a schematic illustration of the possible lubricating mechanism of the Ca(ReO4)2 additive at elevated temperatures is provided from the perspective of structure evolution in Fig. 15 . The Ca(ReO4)2 additive was uniformly dispersed in the base oil, and the resulting mixture was be added onto the contact surface as a functional lubricant. With the rise of the temperature, the base oil began to evaporate and decompose, which reduced its bearing and lubricating capacity. Meanwhile, under pressure and shear action, the Ca(ReO4)2 additive was repeatedly ground and smeared on the worn surface, forming a compact protective layer that contained some metal oxides deriving from the superalloy. The formation of this protective layer was believed to be a result of combined action of several factors, including repeated oxidization, removal, mixing, and reinforcement. Consisting of Ca(ReO4)2 and native metal oxides that had satisfactory bearing and lubricating capacity, the layer was densely distributed on the worn surface and confined friction within its interior, thereby enabling minimized friction.

Nano-sized calcium perrhenate was prepared using the microemulsion reaction method and then, by the aid of surfactant, suspended in PETE base oil to act as a lubricating additive. Tribological tests demonstrated that adding 0.05wt% of our designed calcium perrhenate to the base oil for the steel/steel pair under four ball testing could result in a significant decrease in friction coefficients and WSDs. This may be ascribed to the additive's small size and low shear strength, which allowed it to easily enter the contact area and act as ball bearing at the micro scale. At high temperatures, the lubricating capacity was given by the combined action of the calcium perrhenate and the native metal oxides between the Si3N4/GH4169 contact surfaces. The native metal oxides were hard and brittle in nature, which could act as hard phases to enhance the strength and carrying capacity of the layer. The Ca(ReO4)2 additive played a significant role in friction reduction due to its high thermal stability and its high shear susceptibility under elevated temperatures. Therefore, the Ca(ReO4)2 composite can be a promising candidate as an oil additive well-suited for lubrication applications in a variety of temperature conditions.
The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are very grateful to the financial supports by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51805336, 11604224, 61973224), the Natural Science Foundation of Liaoning Province (20170540740, 20180550861, 2019-ZD-0670), the Scientific Research Project of Education Department of Liaoning Province (lnjc201918), the Tribology Science Fund of State Key Laboratory of Tribology (SKLTKF16B07), the Open Fund of Shenyang Key Laboratory of Aero-engine Materials Tribology (SKLAMT201901).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jmrt.2020.04.048.
